title: Paeonia lactiflora (White Peony) Medicinal Guide
benefits_text: Paeonia lactiflora has several health benefits, such as reducing inflammation and alleviating pain, making it a popular natural remedy for conditions like arthritis and menstrual cramps. Additionally, it improves blood circulation, regulates the menstrual cycle, and soothes skin irritations, making it beneficial for the skin and reproductive system. The herb also has calming properties, promoting better sleep and reducing redness and swelling. Furthermore, Paeonia lactiflora acts as an antioxidant, boosts the immune system, lowers blood pressure, and reduces cholesterol levels, all of which contribute to better cardiovascular health. It also prevents blood clots, protects liver function, and regulates blood sugar, making it a valuable tool in managing diabetes. Moreover, Paeonia lactiflora inhibits tumor growth, slows down the aging process, enhances cognitive function, and even has anti-cancer properties, making it a powerful herb for overall health and well-being.
constituents_text: Paeonia lactiflora has several active constituents, such as Paeoniflorin, Albinum, Oxypeonoside, Benignoside, Lactiflorin, Paeonol, Galactose, Glucose, Sucrose, Beta-sitosterol, Stigmasterol, Lucidic acid, Paeonolic acid, Benzoic acid, Cinnamic acid, Ferulic acid, Salicylic acid, Chlorogenic acid, Kaempferol, Quercetin. These constituents have been found to have medicinal properties, with some acting as antioxidants, anti-inflammatory agents, and analgesics. For example, Paeoniflorin and Paeonol have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, making them useful for treating conditions such as arthritis and muscle cramps. Meanwhile, Galactose, Glucose, and Sucrose provide energy and help regulate bodily functions. Beta-sitosterol and Stigmasterol have been found to have cholesterol-lowering and immune-boosting properties. Lastly, Lucidic acid, Paeonolic acid, Benzoic acid, Cinnamic acid, Ferulic acid, Salicylic acid, Chlorogenic acid, Kaempferol, and Quercetin all have antioxidant properties, which can help protect the body from damage by free radicals and reduce the risk of chronic diseases.
preparations_text: Paeonia lactiflora has several medicinal preparations such as infusions and tinctures which are prepared by steeping the crushed or dried plant in a solvent. Decoctions, fluid extracts, and dry extracts are made by boiling the plant in a liquid and then reducing it down, or by extracting the active compounds in a liquid medium, respectively. Powders, capsules, and tablets are forms of the dried, ground plant, or extracted compounds, often encapsulated for ease of consumption. Topical forms such as ointments, creams, liniments, salves, gels, and poultices are used for local application to the skin, while syrups, lozenges, gargles, and mouthwashes are for oral or throat ailments. Suppositories are used for rectal administration, and eye washes for rinsing the eyes. Each of these preparations allows for the delivery of the medicinal benefits of white peony in a form tailored to the patient's needs.
side_effects_text: Paeonia lactiflora, or white peony, can have side effects if used improperly, and these may include digestive issues such as dry mouth, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Additionally, users may experience neurological symptoms like dizziness, headaches, drowsiness, and fatigue. In some cases, users may also notice allergic reactions, which can present as skin issues like rashes, or more serious symptoms such as numbness and muscle weakness. Furthermore, this herb can also cause menstrual changes and low blood pressure. As with any medication or supplement, it is important to follow dosage instructions carefully and consult with a healthcare provider to ensure its safe use.
precautions_text: Before using Paeonia lactiflora for medicinal purposes, it's important to take some precautions, such as consulting a healthcare professional to ensure its safe for you and to determine the correct dosage. Additionally, pregnant and breastfeeding women should avoid its use, and you should check for any allergies to Paeonia lactiflora before using. Discontinue use if adverse reactions occur, and monitor blood sugar levels, inform healthcare providers of your use, and avoid combining it with blood-thinning medications. Limit use to 3-6 weeks for continuous treatment, and do not use in children under 12 years old. Additionally, monitor liver function during use, limit alcohol consumption while using, and do not consume it with grapefruit or grapefruit juice. Discontinue use before surgery, and consult a healthcare professional before using for children and for specific medical conditions. Always follow dosage instructions carefully, store in a cool, dry place away from sunlight, and keep it out of reach of children and pets. Do not use if the seal is broken or missing.
benefits_list: 1. Reduces inflammation: Paeonia lactiflora, or Chinese peony, has anti-inflammatory properties that can help lessen inflammation in the body.
2. Alleviates pain: This herb has analgesic effects, making it useful for relieving various types of pain.
3. Improves blood circulation: Paeonia lactiflora can help improve blood flow, which may benefit overall health and well-being.
4. Regulates menstrual cycle: This herb has been used in traditional Chinese medicine to help regulate menstrual cycles and address related hormonal imbalances.
5. Relieves menstrual cramps: Paeonia lactiflora may help alleviate menstrual cramps by reducing inflammation and spasms in the uterus.
6. Eases symptoms of PMS: By regulating hormones and reducing inflammation, this herb can help alleviate common PMS symptoms like bloating, mood swings, and breast tenderness.
7. Soothes skin irritations: Paeonia lactiflora has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe skin irritations and reduce redness.
8. Reduces redness and swelling: This herb's anti-inflammatory effects can help reduce redness and swelling in various parts of the body.
9. Calms the nervous system: Paeonia lactiflora has calming properties that can help promote relaxation and reduce anxiety.
10. Promotes better sleep: By calming the nervous system and reducing inflammation, this herb may help improve sleep quality and duration.
constituents_list: 1. Paeoniflorin: A monoterpene glucoside with anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties.
2. Albinum: A chemical compound with limited research, suggested to have anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects.
3. Oxypeonoside: A phenolic glycoside that may have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neuroprotective properties.
4. Benignoside: A glucoside with potential cardiovascular benefits, such as reducing blood pressure and protecting against oxidative stress.
5. Lactiflorin: A glycoside found in Paeonia lactiflora with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.
6. Paeonol: A phenolic compound with anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic properties, also known for its potential neuroprotective effects.
7. Galactose: A simple sugar that serves as a fundamental building block for more complex carbohydrates and has potential antioxidant properties.
8. Glucose: A simple sugar that serves as a primary energy source for cells and is involved in various metabolic processes.
9. Sucrose: A disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose, serving as a common sweetener and energy source.
10. Beta-sitosterol: A plant sterol with potential cholesterol-lowering and anti-inflammatory effects, also known for its potential immunomodulatory properties.
preparations_list: 1. Infusion: A preparation made by steeping Paeonia lactiflora in hot water, often used for its soothing effects on the digestive and nervous systems.
2. Tincture: A concentrated liquid extract of Paeonia lactiflora, preserved in alcohol, taken in small doses for its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties.
3. Decoction: A strong, concentrated liquid preparation of Paeonia lactiflora, made by boiling and simmering the root, used for its antispasmodic and sedative effects.
4. Fluid extract: A highly concentrated, liquid form of Paeonia lactiflora, prepared by percolation, and used for its medicinal properties in a smaller dose than a decoction.
5. Dry extract: A concentrated, powdered extract of Paeonia lactiflora, prepared by evaporating the liquid extract, and used for its medicinal benefits in a more convenient and stable form.
6. Powder: A finely ground, dry form of Paeonia lactiflora, taken in small doses for its anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and sedative effects.
7. Capsules: Encapsulated, dry extract or powder of Paeonia lactiflora, taken orally for its medicinal benefits, allowing for precise dosing and convenient administration.
8. Tablets: Compressed, dry extract or powder of Paeonia lactiflora, taken orally for its medicinal benefits, allowing for precise dosing and convenient administration.
9. Ointment: A semi-solid preparation of Paeonia lactiflora, mixed with a base, used topically for its soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on the skin.
10. Cream: A water-in-oil emulsion of Paeonia lactiflora, used topically for its soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on the skin, and for its ability to improve skin texture and tone.
side_effects_list: 1. Digestive issues: May cause stomach discomfort, cramps, or irregular bowel movements.
2. Dizziness: May experience lightheadedness or a feeling of spinning.
3. Headache: May result in pulsating head pain or migraine.
4. Rash: Can cause skin irritation, itchiness, or breakouts.
5. Dry mouth: May lead to constant thirst or difficulty swallowing.
6. Nausea: Can result in stomach discomfort and an urge to vomit.
7. Vomiting: May cause forceful stomach ejection of food or fluids.
8. Diarrhea: Can result in loose, watery, or frequent bowel movements.
9. Drowsiness: May lead to sleepiness, lethargy, or reduced alertness.
10. Fatigue: Can result in feeling overly tired, weak, or lacking energy.
precautions_list: 1. Consult a healthcare professional before use: It is important to seek advice from a medical professional to ensure safety and effectiveness of using Paeonia lactiflora for specific health conditions.
2. Determine correct dosage with a healthcare provider: A healthcare provider can help determine the appropriate dosage based on factors such as age, health status, and other medications being taken.
3. Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding: Paeonia lactiflora may have hormonal effects and is best avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding due to potential risks to the infant.
4. Check for allergies to Peonia lactiflora: Discontinue use and seek medical attention if any allergic reactions occur, such as rash, itching, or difficulty breathing.
5. Discontinue use if adverse reactions occur: Stop using Paeonia lactiflora and consult a healthcare provider if any negative side effects are experienced.
6. Monitor blood sugar levels closely: Paeonia lactiflora may lower blood sugar levels, so it is important to monitor blood sugar levels closely and adjust medication dosages as needed.

7. Inform healthcare providers of Peonia lactiflora use: It is important to inform healthcare providers of any herbal supplements being taken, including Paeonia lactiflora, to avoid potential interactions with other medications.
8. Avoid combining with blood-thinning medications: Paeonia lactiflora may increase the risk of bleeding when combined with blood-thinning medications, so it is important to avoid this combination.
9. Limit use to 3-6 weeks for continuous treatment: Prolonged use of Paeonia lactiflora may lead to potential side effects, so it is recommended to limit use to 3-6 weeks for continuous treatment.

10. Do not use in children under 12 years old: The safety and effectiveness of Paeonia lactiflora in children under 12 years old has not been established, so it is best to avoid its use in this population.
